3D Printing: Additive manufacturing producers and platform vendors building hardware, materials, and software for industrial 3D printing. Constituents include 3D Systems, Materialise, Velo3D, and Nano Dimension. Drivers center on aerospace and medical-device adoption, capital-equipment spend, materials science, and the ongoing shift from prototyping to series production.
3D printing: Additive manufacturing vendors building hardware and software for industrial 3D printing, represented here by Nano Dimension and Materialise. Drivers include aerospace and medical-device qualification cycles, materials development, and the slow but persistent push from prototyping into low-volume serial production.
AI - Autonomous Systems: Small-cap exposure to autonomous-systems hardware and software, including chip-interconnect supplier Valens Semiconductor and autonomy software firm Cyngn. Drivers include defense and industrial robotics demand, ADAS regulatory tightening, and the cost curve of perception and compute stacks needed for unsupervised operation.
AI - Customer Service: Conversational-AI and contact-center software providers serving enterprise customer-service workloads, including 8x8, eGain, and LivePerson. Key drivers are enterprise IT consolidation onto AI-native call-handling, attrition pressure on traditional BPO labor pools, and integration with large-language-model APIs for voice and chat.
AI Chips: Semiconductor companies whose data-center and accelerator products supply the AI training and inference build-out. Constituents are NVIDIA, Broadcom, AMD, and Intel. Drivers include hyperscaler capex cycles, networking-silicon attach rates, advanced-packaging supply, and the competitive trajectory of GPU vs. custom ASIC architectures.
AI Chips - Startup: Smaller semiconductor names positioned as next-generation AI compute and connectivity, including photonic-interconnect firm POET Technologies and ultra-low-power inference designer Ambiq Micro. Drivers include hyperscaler diversification away from incumbents, edge-AI device penetration, and the funding climate for fabless startups.
AI Data Center + Bitcoin Miner: Bitcoin-mining operators repositioning a portion of their power and real-estate footprint into AI compute hosting. Includes TeraWulf, Cipher, Riot Platforms, Core Scientific, and CleanSpark. Drivers include hyperscaler search for power-secured sites, BTC price and difficulty, and the economics of repurposed mining halls into liquid-cooled GPU bays.
AI Data Center - Large: Pure-play AI compute hosts and neoclouds with sizeable installed GPU bases, including CoreWeave, Nebius, IREN, and Applied Digital. Drivers include hyperscaler take-or-pay contracts, GPU supply allocation, electricity and grid-interconnect availability, and customer concentration risk.
AI Drug Discovery: Biotechs and software platforms applying machine learning to molecule generation, target selection, and trial design. Constituents include Recursion Pharmaceuticals, AbCellera, Certara, and Schrödinger. Drivers include pharma R&D-services demand, partnership economics, and the slow translation of in-silico hit rates into clinical-stage validation.
AI Security - China: China-listed AI vision and security software exposure, anchored by SenseTime. Drivers include domestic surveillance and smart-city contracts, U.S. entity-list sanctions and export controls, and Beijing's policy stance toward private AI champions.
AI Software: Mid- and small-cap pure-play AI software vendors selling enterprise and government deployments, including SoundHound AI, BigBear.ai, C3.ai, and Rezolve AI. Drivers include enterprise pilot-to-production conversion rates, federal AI-budget allocations, and pricing power as foundation-model costs decline.
AI Software - Large: Large-cap enterprise software incumbents whose AI features are central to revenue narratives, including Palantir, Salesforce, and ServiceNow. Drivers include seat-attach economics, agent-based product launches, and customers' willingness to pay premium prices for embedded AI inside existing workflows.
AI Software - Small: Small-cap AI-themed software names spanning data infrastructure, voice AI, and analytics, including Datavault AI, Cerence, Gorilla Technology, Alpha Technology Group, and Veritone. Drivers include narrow vertical wins, customer concentration risk, and capital-raising conditions for unprofitable issuers.
AI Software - Tiny: Micro-cap AI exposure represented here by Marchex, an advertising-call analytics provider applying AI to call-tracking and conversation intelligence. Drivers include local-ads spending cycles, conversational-AI feature adoption inside legacy ad-tech, and small-cap liquidity conditions.
AI data center + bitcoin miner: Bitcoin miners repurposing power-rich infrastructure for AI compute hosting, including TeraWulf, Riot, Core Scientific, and CleanSpark. Drivers include hyperscaler interest in power-secured sites, BTC price and hashrate, and conversion economics from ASIC mining to GPU hosting.
AI data center - large: Large neocloud and AI hosting operators with material installed GPU capacity, including CoreWeave, Nebius, IREN, and Applied Digital. Drivers include hyperscaler take-or-pay contracts, GPU allocation, power-interconnect timelines, and customer concentration.
AI drug discovery: Companies applying machine learning to drug discovery, including Recursion, AbCellera, Certara, and Schrödinger. Drivers include pharma R&D-services demand, structure of partnership economics, and the rate at which in-silico predictions translate into clinical-stage validation.
AI software - large: Large-cap enterprise software platforms with central AI revenue narratives, including Palantir, Salesforce, and ServiceNow. Drivers include AI-feature attach rates, agent-product traction, and willingness of customers to pay incrementally for AI inside existing workflows.
AI-Powered Printing & Tools: Industrial printing and digital-fabrication firms applying automation and AI to manufacturing workflows, including Xometry, Proto Labs, Eastman Kodak, Cricut, Stratasys, and Kornit Digital. Drivers include reshoring of short-run manufacturing, custom-textile demand, and on-demand industrial parts marketplaces.
APM & data security: Application-performance monitoring and data-security platform vendors, including Datadog, Rubrik, Dynatrace, Commvault, and PagerDuty. Drivers include cloud workload growth, AI-observability spend, ransomware-driven backup adoption, and consolidation of point tools onto unified observability platforms.
ARK ETF Trust: Holdings sleeve referencing the ARK Invest thematic ETF complex — Innovation, Genomic Revolution, Autonomous Tech & Robotics, Next Generation Internet, and Space & Defense Innovation. Drivers reflect ARK's disruptive-tech thesis, fund-flow dynamics, and underlying performance of ARK's high-conviction names.
ATM & Retail Machines: Self-service retail and banking hardware vendors, including NCR Atleos, Diebold Nixdorf, Crane NXT, NCR Voyix, and Datalogic. Drivers include cash-cycle automation contracts with banks, retail point-of-sale refresh cycles, and the long-running secular debate over cash usage in developed markets.
ATM & retail macines: Self-service ATM and retail-hardware vendors, including NCR Atleos, Diebold Nixdorf, Crane NXT, and NCR Voyix. Drivers include cash-cycle outsourcing contracts with banks, retail point-of-sale refresh cycles, and trends in cash usage versus contactless payment.
Ad Agency - Big-4: Global advertising holding companies — Omnicom, Publicis, and WPP — selling integrated creative, media-buying, and data services to multinational advertisers. Drivers include client account moves, generative-AI disruption to creative workflows, agency consolidation, and the pace of digital ad-spend recovery.
Adhesive: Specialty-chemical adhesive and protective-coating producers, including H.B. Fuller, Avient, WD-40, and Koppers. Drivers include packaging and construction end-market demand, raw-material input costs, formulation IP, and pricing discipline within a consolidated supplier base.
Adhesive & Label: Adhesive, label, and specialty-packaging producers serving consumer and industrial customers, including Nitto Denko, Avery Dennison, Solventum, CCL Industries, and Kanadevia. Drivers include consumer-staples volumes, e-commerce shipping label demand, materials cost, and label-automation in food and pharma.
Advertising - Outdoor: Out-of-home advertising operators owning billboards, digital displays, and transit ad inventory, including Lamar Advertising, OUTFRONT Media, JCDecaux, Ströer, and Clear Channel Outdoor. Drivers include the pace of static-to-digital screen conversion, programmatic OOH adoption, urban foot-traffic, and balance-sheet leverage.
Advertising Agencies - China: China-listed digital ad and content names with advertising-agency exposure, including Xunlei and Cheetah Mobile. Drivers include domestic ad-spend cycles, regulatory posture toward gaming and content platforms, and the listing-status overhang from U.S.-China audit and delisting frictions.
Aerospace - Others: Smaller aerospace-adjacent names including A2Z Cust2Mate Solutions, AerSale, and GrabAGun. Drivers include after-market parts demand, defense-procurement cycles, and small-cap funding conditions.
Aerospace - Parts & Repair: Mid-sized aerospace parts and aftermarket-repair specialists, including Hexcel (composite materials), Mercury Systems (defense electronics), and AAR Corp. (MRO services). Drivers include narrowbody build rates at Boeing and Airbus, defense electronics content per platform, and global airline maintenance spending.
Aerospace Electronics: Avionics and defense-electronics suppliers, including Astronics, Redwire, Ducommun, and Momentus. Drivers include defense-budget growth, space-domain investment, electronic-warfare programs, and platform-content gains on next-generation aircraft.
Aerospace Parts: Mid-cap aerospace structural and component suppliers, including Karman Holdings, StandardAero, Loar Holdings, VSE Corporation, and SIFCO Industries. Drivers include OEM build-rate ramps at Boeing and Airbus, MRO demand for aging fleets, and defense aftermarket growth.
Aerospace Parts & Structures: Large aerospace structural and content suppliers, including Howmet Aerospace, TransDigm, L3Harris, HEICO, and Curtiss-Wright. Drivers include OEM production ramps, aftermarket pricing power, defense electronics content per platform, and consolidation among Tier 1 suppliers.
Aerospace Parts - Flow & Control: Aerospace flow-control and motion-systems suppliers, including Woodward, Leonardo DRS, Moog, and CAE. Drivers include OEM rate ramps, defense-electronics content growth, training and simulation contract wins, and aftermarket service intensity.
Aerospace Parts - Small: Smaller aerospace component suppliers, including Graham Corporation, Park Aerospace, and TAT Technologies. Drivers include OEM rate ramps, defense-program awards, and aftermarket spare-parts demand on aging fleets.
Aesthetic Medical Products: Aesthetic-medicine device and product makers, including InMode, Evolus, So-Young International, and Cutera. Drivers include consumer discretionary spending on cosmetic procedures, competitive launches in neurotoxins and energy-based devices, and reimbursement and regulatory dynamics in major markets.
Air & Water Treatment: Filtration and pollution-control equipment makers, including Atmus Filtration, CECO Environmental, Select Water Solutions, and PureCycle Technologies. Drivers include diesel-fleet emissions standards, industrial water-reuse mandates, oil-and-gas water-management spending, and plastics-recycling capacity build-out.
Air Services - Canada: Canadian airline and air-cargo operators, including Air Canada, Cargojet, Chorus Aviation, and Transat. Drivers include cross-border passenger demand, fuel costs, e-commerce parcel volumes for Cargojet, and CAD/USD currency translation.
Airlines - Europe: Major European passenger airlines, including Ryanair, IAG, Lufthansa, easyJet, and Air France-KLM. Drivers include fuel hedging, intra-European leisure demand, slot allocation at congested hubs, labor disputes, and fleet-renewal capex with Boeing and Airbus.
Airlines - Europe - Small: Smaller European carriers, including Jet2, Wizz Air, and Finnair. Drivers include leisure-travel demand on package-holiday and Central/Eastern European routes, fuel hedging, fleet-rotation costs, and competitive capacity additions in low-cost segments.
Airlines - Japan: Japan's two flag carriers, ANA Holdings and Japan Airlines. Drivers include international long-haul recovery, inbound-tourism flows, JPY exchange rate effects on fuel and capex, and competition from low-cost carriers on regional routes.
Airlines - Latin America: Latin American passenger carriers, including LATAM, Copa Holdings, Aeromexico, and Volaris (Vuela). Drivers include cross-border travel demand, USD-denominated fuel and aircraft costs, regional currency volatility, and capacity discipline after pandemic-era restructurings.
Airlines - Southwest Asia: Asia-Pacific full-service carriers, including Singapore Airlines, Cathay Pacific, Qantas, and Thai Airways. Drivers include long-haul premium-cabin demand, China outbound recovery, fuel costs, and regional currency translation against the U.S. dollar.
Airlines - US - Medium: Mid-sized U.S. passenger airlines, including SkyWest, JetBlue, Allegiant, Republic, and Frontier. Drivers include domestic leisure-travel demand, regional-pilot supply, fuel costs, and the cost gap versus the legacy big-three carriers.
Airlines - USA - Big5: Largest U.S. passenger airlines — Delta, United, Southwest, American, and Alaska. Drivers include corporate and premium-leisure demand, fuel hedging, labor contract resets, capacity discipline among the big four, and loyalty-program economics.
Airplane Manufacturer: North American business and regional aircraft OEMs, Bombardier and Textron. Drivers include corporate-jet order books, defense and trainer-aircraft contracts, after-market services growth, and supply-chain capacity for engine and structural components.
Airports - Large: Large publicly traded airport operators, including Aena, Airports of Thailand, Aeroports de Paris, Flughafen Zürich, Auckland International, and Fraport. Drivers include passenger throughput, regulated aero-charge resets, retail and parking concession yields, and capex on terminal expansions.
Aluminum: Mid-cap aluminum producers, including Alcoa, South32, Century Aluminum, Constellium, and Kaiser Aluminum. Drivers include LME aluminum pricing, U.S. and EU tariff regimes, energy costs at smelters, demand from auto-light-weighting and packaging, and trade flows from Chinese exports.
Aluminum - Large: Large international aluminum producers, including China Hongqiao, Aluminum Corporation of China (Chalco), and Norsk Hydro. Drivers include LME pricing, smelter energy costs, alumina supply, China export-quota policy, and demand from electrification and packaging end-markets.
Analog Chips: Analog and mixed-signal semiconductor makers serving industrial, automotive, and communications markets, including Texas Instruments, Analog Devices, ON Semiconductor, and MACOM Technology. Drivers include automotive content per vehicle, industrial-capex cycles, inventory normalization, and the slow capacity ramp at trailing-node fabs.
Animal Health - Major: Large animal-health pharma and diagnostics firms, Zoetis, IDEXX Laboratories, and Elanco. Drivers include companion-animal care spending, U.S. veterinary visit trends, livestock vaccine demand, parasiticide launches, and pricing power within a concentrated industry.
Animal Health - Medium: Mid-cap animal-health and adjacent diagnostics firms, including Sotera Health, Phibro Animal Health, Neogen, and PetMed Express. Drivers include veterinary-services spend, livestock vaccine cycles, food-safety testing demand, and online pet-pharmacy competition.
Annuity (Spinoff): Insurance-spinoff annuity and life specialists, including Corebridge Financial, Jackson Financial, F&G Annuities & Life, and Brighthouse Financial. Drivers include 10-year Treasury yields, retail annuity sales volumes, hedging effectiveness on variable-annuity guarantees, and capital returns from parent-spin balance sheets.
Antibodies: Clinical-stage biotechs developing antibody-based therapies, including Edgewise Therapeutics, Relay Therapeutics, Celldex Therapeutics, and Xencor. Drivers include trial-readout binary events, partnership economics with large pharma, biotech XBI sentiment, and intellectual-property positioning around antibody engineering.
Antibody Testing: Diagnostics and assay providers focused on antibody and genetic testing, including OPKO Health, Pacific Biosciences, Fulgent Genetics, and Myriad Genetics. Drivers include reimbursement coverage decisions, sequencing-instrument placements, oncology and reproductive-health test volumes, and competitive pressure from large laboratory networks.
Antibody-Based Products: Mid-cap biotechs commercializing antibody-based therapeutics, including Genmab, Ascendis Pharma, Exelixis, and BioMarin. Drivers include royalty streams from partnered programs, label-expansion approvals, biosimilar competition, and pipeline cadence in oncology and rare disease.
Apartment REIT: U.S. multifamily apartment REITs, including Independence Realty, Veris Residential, NexPoint Residential, Apartment Investment & Management, and Elme Communities. Drivers include Sun Belt versus coastal rent-growth divergence, supply pipelines, mortgage-rate effects on for-sale alternatives, and operating-expense inflation.
Apartment REIT - Canada: Canadian multi-residential REITs, including CAPREIT, Boardwalk, Killam, InterRent, Morguard North American Residential, and Minto Apartment. Drivers include immigration-driven rental demand, rent-control regimes (notably Ontario), CAD interest-rate cycle, and disposition pricing in major metros.
Apparel & Textile - Essential: Apparel and textile manufacturers serving basics and uniforms, including Gildan Activewear, Shenzhou International, V.F. Corporation, and PVH Corp. Drivers include cotton input prices, sourcing-country tariff exposure, brand-portfolio mix, and wholesale-versus-DTC channel economics.
Apparel - Fast Fashion: Vertically integrated fast-fashion retailers, including Inditex (Zara), Fast Retailing (Uniqlo), H&M, and Gap. Drivers include speed of design-to-shelf cycles, shipping costs from Asia sourcing hubs, store productivity in core markets, and competitive pressure from Shein and Temu.
Apparel - Jeans: Denim-focused apparel brands, Levi Strauss, Kontoor Brands (Wrangler, Lee), and American Eagle Outfitters. Drivers include cotton-input cost, wholesale-versus-DTC channel mix, premium-denim cycles, and U.S. mall-traffic trends.
Apparel - Luxury: European luxury apparel and accessories brands, Moncler, Prada, and Burberry. Drivers include Chinese consumer demand and outbound travel, USD/EUR translation effects, brand-pricing power versus aspirational customers, and inventory discipline in wholesale channels.
Apparel - Premium: Premium fashion and accessible-luxury brands, Tapestry (Coach, Kate Spade), Ralph Lauren, and Capri Holdings (Michael Kors, Versace, Jimmy Choo). Drivers include North American handbag demand, China traffic, brand elevation strategies, and outcome of pending consolidation reviews.
Apparel - Sports: Global sports and athleisure brands, NIKE, adidas, Amer Sports (Arc'teryx, Salomon), ASICS, and lululemon athletica. Drivers include innovation cadence in footwear, China and DTC channel performance, on-trend running-shoe momentum, and inventory discipline.
Apparel - Sports - China: China-domiciled sportswear brands, ANTA Sports, Li Ning, and Topsports International. Drivers include domestic consumer-discretionary spending, brand-portfolio resonance with younger consumers, retail-network productivity, and policy posture toward consumption stimulus.
Apparel Brands & Retail: Mid-cap specialty apparel retailers, Aritzia, Urban Outfitters, Abercrombie & Fitch, and The Buckle. Drivers include U.S. mall and digital traffic, brand-elevation execution, inventory turns, and ability to pass through supplier-cost inflation.
Apparel Manufacturing - Small: Small-cap apparel manufacturers and DTC retailers, G-III Apparel, Oxford Industries, Stitch Fix, Torrid, and Xcel Brands. Drivers include licensed-brand portfolio shifts, full-price sell-through, subscription-retention for Stitch Fix, and inventory-clearance pressure.
Apparel Retail - Off-Price: Off-price apparel and home retailers, TJX Companies, Ross Stores, Burlington Stores, and Vipshop Holdings. Drivers include availability of branded closeout inventory, traffic versus full-price competitors, store-expansion runway, and the resilience of value-seeking consumer behavior.
Apparel Retail - UK: UK apparel retailers, Frasers Group, THG, and ASOS. Drivers include UK consumer discretionary spending, online return-rate economics, M&A among legacy high-street brands, and GBP currency translation.
Appliance: Major home and small-appliance brands, De'Longhi, Alliance Laundry, Whirlpool, Breville, SEB, and AB Electrolux. Drivers include U.S. and EU housing turnover, replacement-cycle timing, raw-material costs, and tariff pass-through on imported appliances.
Appliance - International - Large: Large international consumer-electronics and appliance manufacturers, Panasonic Holdings and Haier Smart Home. Drivers include global housing markets, appliance-replacement cycles, smart-appliance attach rates, and yen and yuan currency translation.
Asset Management - Australia: Australian asset managers, Perpetual, Magellan Financial, and Insignia Financial. Drivers include net flows in active equity strategies, AUD market levels, super-fund consolidation pressure, and competition from passive products.
Asset Management - Batch2: Diversified U.S. asset and wealth managers, Northern Trust, T. Rowe Price, Franklin Resources, and Invesco. Drivers include market levels, active-versus-passive flow trends, fee compression, and capital-return policies.
Asset Management - Europe: European asset-management groups, Amundi and Groupe Bruxelles Lambert. Drivers include European retail flows, ETF and ESG product launches, AUM-fee mix, and capital deployment by holding-company structures.
Asset Management - Large: Largest U.S. asset and custody franchises, BlackRock, Bank of New York Mellon, Brookfield Asset Management, and State Street. Drivers include ETF and private-markets flow share, custody fee economics, capital returns, and AI-driven cost productivity.
Asset Management - Medium: Mid-cap U.S. asset managers, Affiliated Managers Group, AllianceBernstein, Cohen & Steers, and GCM Grosvenor. Drivers include net flows, fee-rate mix, performance fees in alternatives, and incentive-comp leverage to AUM growth.
Asset Management - Medium - 2: Mid-cap asset managers, Janus Henderson, Victory Capital, Federated Hermes, Sprott, and Artisan Partners. Drivers include net flows in active equity and fixed income, money-market fee waivers when rates fall, and acquisition cadence among aggregator platforms.
Asset Management - Medium - 3: Mid-cap asset and wealth platforms, WisdomTree, Acadian Asset Management, Wealthfront, and General American Investors. Drivers include ETF flow share, robo-advisory net deposits, market levels, and AUM-fee mix.
Asset Management - Medium - 4: Smaller diversified asset and multi-family-office firms, Virtus Investment Partners and AlTi Global. Drivers include net flows by strategy, fee-rate mix, and acquisition cadence in the wealth and aggregator landscape.
Asset Management - Switzerland: Swiss private banks and asset managers, Julius Bär, EFG International, and Vontobel Holding. Drivers include high-net-worth client inflows, CHF strength, transaction-fee activity, and regulatory and reputational risk from cross-border banking.
Asset Management - UK: UK asset and wealth managers, Legal & General, Schroders, M&G, St. James's Place, and Jupiter Fund Management. Drivers include UK retirement-product flows, advised-platform consolidation, regulatory action on advice fees, and GBP-denominated AUM dynamics.
Assisted Living: Skilled-nursing and assisted-living operators, The Ensign Group, PACS Group, and Chemed. Drivers include Medicaid and Medicare reimbursement, occupancy recovery, labor costs and immigration policy, and demographic-driven demand from aging cohorts.
Auto & Parts - Japan: Japanese mid-cap auto manufacturers, Subaru and Mazda. Drivers include U.S. light-vehicle demand (their largest market), JPY exchange rates, hybrid-and-EV product cadence, and competitive pressure from Chinese OEMs in adjacent markets.
Auto Dealership: U.S. franchised auto dealer networks, Penske, AutoNation, Lithia Motors, Group 1, and Asbury Automotive. Drivers include new and used vehicle gross-profit per unit, finance-and-insurance penetration, store-acquisition pace, and floor-plan financing costs.
Auto Manufacturers - China: China's leading domestic auto OEMs, Geely Automobile and Great Wall Motor. Drivers include domestic price-war intensity, NEV penetration, export-sales growth, government subsidy and tariff policy, and competitive positioning versus BYD.
Auto Manufacturers - Geely: Geely-affiliated EV and luxury marques, Volvo Cars, Polestar, Lotus Technology, and ECARX. Drivers include China-Europe trade-tariff dynamics, EV demand in Europe, capital adequacy at the loss-making EV brands, and Geely group's funding posture.
Auto Manufacturers - Germany: German legacy OEMs, BMW, Volkswagen, and Mercedes-Benz. Drivers include China premium-segment demand, EV transition costs, EU emissions targets, and competitive pressure from Chinese OEMs entering Europe.
Auto Manufacturers - India: India's largest listed auto OEM, Mahindra & Mahindra, with broad SUV and farm-equipment exposure. Drivers include domestic SUV demand, monsoon-driven rural cycles for farm equipment, EV product launches, and competitive intensity from Tata Motors and Maruti Suzuki.
Auto Manufacturers - Japan & France: Honda, Renault, and Nissan — the three names tied to the historical Renault-Nissan alliance and Honda's separate global franchise. Drivers include hybrid-product cadence, alliance-restructuring decisions, EV strategy, and JPY/EUR currency translation.
Auto Manufacturers - Japan - Medium: Mid-cap Japanese OEMs, Suzuki Motor, Isuzu Motors, Yamaha Motor, and Mitsubishi Motors. Drivers include India and ASEAN demand for Suzuki, commercial-vehicle cycles for Isuzu, motorcycle and marine engine sales for Yamaha, and JPY translation effects.
Auto Manufacturers - US - Big3: Detroit Three OEMs, GM, Ford, and Stellantis. Drivers include U.S. light-truck demand, EV transition capex versus pull-back decisions, UAW labor cost trajectory, and tariff regime on cross-border supply chains.
Auto Parts - Axle: Driveline and powertrain-component suppliers, Allison Transmission, Melrose Industries, Dana, and Dauch Corporation (formerly American Axle). Drivers include light- and commercial-vehicle build rates, EV-platform content shifts, aftermarket pricing, and OEM consolidation pressure on suppliers.
Auto Parts - Europe: European tier-one auto-parts suppliers, Schaeffler, Valeo, Forvia, and Peugeot Invest. Drivers include European OEM build rates, EV content shifts, restructuring execution at Forvia and Valeo, and competitive pricing pressure as automakers cut costs.
Auto Parts - Japan: Large Japanese auto-parts suppliers, DENSO and Aisin. Drivers include Toyota build rates, hybrid-component demand, EV-platform content, JPY translation, and supplier-OEM cost-sharing dynamics.
Auto Parts - Safety Systems: Auto safety-systems and interior suppliers, Autoliv, Gentex, Dorman Products, Visteon, Adient, and Gentherm. Drivers include global light-vehicle production, ADAS-content growth per vehicle, EV-cabin electronics adoption, and aftermarket replacement-parts demand.
Auto Parts Manufacturers: Diversified tier-one auto-parts suppliers, Magna International, Aptiv, BorgWarner, Lear, and Linamar. Drivers include OEM build rates, EV-platform content versus ICE, contract-manufacturing wins for Magna and Aptiv, and labor- and material-cost pass-through.
Auto Retail - China: China-listed online auto retail and information platforms, Autohome and Uxin. Drivers include domestic auto-sales cycles, used-car demand and price levels, advertiser spend by OEMs, and competitive pressure from short-video and super-app channels.
Auto Retails - Auction: Online auto-marketplace and wholesale-auction operators, CarGurus, ACV Auctions, and Cars.com. Drivers include used-vehicle wholesale pricing, dealer subscription-fee economics, lead-conversion rates, and competitive pressure from OEM-direct online sales.
Auto Service: Auto service and reconditioning chains, Valvoline, Boyd Group, Mister Car Wash, Driven Brands, and Monro. Drivers include miles driven, vehicle-fleet age, oil-change interval cadence, and small-bolt-on acquisition pace by the multi-banner chains.
Autoimmune TYK2: Clinical-stage biotechs developing TYK2 and adjacent immunology assets, Alumis, Nektar Therapeutics, and Mesoblast. Drivers include trial-readout binary outcomes in psoriasis and other autoimmune indications, partnership economics, and biotech sentiment.
Cannabis: Cannabis-sector exposure spanning U.S. and Canadian licensed producers, retailers, and ancillary services. Constituents include Curaleaf, Green Thumb Industries, Trulieve, Tilray, and Canopy Growth. Key drivers are state-level legalization, federal banking-reform timelines, tax-rate normalization, and consolidation across the multi-state operator landscape.
cannabis: Cannabis-sector exposure spanning U.S. and Canadian licensed producers, retailers, and ancillary services. Constituents include Curaleaf, Green Thumb Industries, Trulieve, Tilray, and Canopy Growth. Key drivers are state-level legalization, federal banking-reform timelines, tax-rate normalization, and consolidation across the multi-state operator landscape.
